results.label: German hospital — multi-stage actor status transition (MDR 5(5) + AI Act provider/deployer)
results.reliance: Cannot be relied on without major revision
results.answer:
> ASSESSMENT STATUS — Provisional legal qualification assessment. This answer should be read as a case-specific compliance analysis, not as an automatic status determination. > ⚠ BACKBONE FLAG — This answer may incorrectly classify the original AI system developer as a deployer. Under Article 3(3) AI Act, development + internal deployment = provider status from inception. Verify the initial-status analysis before relying on this answer. ### 1. Initial Stage (Development & Internal Use Under MDR Article 5(5)) #### MDR 2017/745 - Actor Status: The German university hospital is a manufacturer under **Article 2(30)** of the MDR because it designs and manufactures the AI-assisted pathology system for its own use. - Exemption Applicability: The hospital qualifies for the **Article 5(5)** health institution exemption because: - The device is manufactured and used only within the hospital (no transfer to another legal entity). - The hospital justifies that no equivalent device meets the specific clinical needs (**Article 5(5)**(c)). - The hospital maintains appropriate quality management systems (**Article 5(5)**(b)) and documentation (**Article 5(5)**(f)). - The device is not placed on the market (no commercial distribution). - Obligations Under Exemption: - The hospital must comply with **Annex I** (General Safety and Performance Requirements). - It must document the clinical justification for in-house manufacturing (**Article 5(5)**(c)). - It must maintain technical documentation and post-market surveillance (**Article 5(5)**(f), (h)). - The exemption does not apply if the device is transferred to another legal entity (**Article 5(5)**(a)). #### EU AI Act - Actor Status: The hospital is a provider under **Article 3(3)** of the AI Act because: - It develops the AI system and puts it into service under its own name. - The AI Act does not have an equivalent in-house exemption—even if the system is used internally, the hospital remains a provider with full obligations. - Classification Route: - Route I (**Article 6(1)** + **Annex I**): The AI system is itself a medical device (MDR-regulated product) and is required to undergo third-party conformity assessment (since it is likely Class IIa/IIb/III under MDR). - Conclusion: The system is high-risk under the AI Act because it meets both conditions of **Article 6(1)**: 1. It is a product covered by MDR (**Annex I**, Section A, point 11). 2. It is subject to third-party conformity assessment under MDR. - Obligations as Provider: - Risk management system (**Article 9**). - Data governance (**Article 10**). - Technical documentation (**Article 11**). - Conformity assessment (**Article 43(3)**—MDR conformity assessment applies, with additional AI Act requirements). - Post-market monitoring (**Article 72**). - EU declaration of conformity (**Article 47**). #### Cross-Regulatory Chain - The MDR exemption (**Article 5(5)**) does not exempt the hospital from AI Act obligations—it remains a high-risk AI provider with full compliance requirements. - The MDR conformity assessment (for the medical device) integrates AI Act requirements (**Article 43(3)**). --- ### 2. Stage 2 (Continuous Learning Pipeline Introduced) #### MDR 2017/745 - Impact of Continuous Learning: - The **Article 5(5)** exemption remains valid as long as: - The retraining is pre-defined in the initial technical documentation. - The validation thresholds and spectral domains are pre-specified (no substantial modification). - The device remains used only within the hospital (no transfer to another legal entity). - If the retraining introduces new intended purposes or significant changes, the hospital must: - Reassess compliance with **Annex I** (safety/performance). - Update clinical evaluation (**Article 61**). - Potentially lose the exemption if the changes are substantial (e.g., new clinical claims, altered risk profile). #### EU AI Act - Impact of Continuous Learning: - The provider status remains unchanged (the hospital is still the developer and user). - High-risk classification persists because: - The AI system remains a medical device (MDR-regulated). - The third-party conformity assessment requirement still applies. - Additional Obligations: - Post-market monitoring must track performance drift and bias (**Article 72**). - Technical documentation must be updated to reflect retraining logic (**Article 11**). - Corrective actions must be taken if the system deviates from compliance (**Article 20**). - no clear transition to deployer status—the hospital remains the provider because it controls the retraining pipeline. #### Cross-Regulatory Chain - The MDR exemption (**Article 5(5)**) is not affected if the retraining is pre-defined and non-substantial. - The AI Act obligations (e.g., post-market monitoring, documentation updates) continue to apply regardless of the MDR exemption. --- ### 3. Stage 3 (Collaboration with French Hospital – Model Weights Shared) #### MDR 2017/745 - Transfer of Model Weights = Transfer to Another Legal Entity: - **Article 5(5)**(a) explicitly states that the exemption does not apply if the device is transferred to another legal entity. - Effect: - The German hospital loses the **Article 5(5)** exemption and becomes a full MDR manufacturer. - It must now comply with all MDR requirements, including: - Conformity assessment (likely involving a notified body). - CE marking. - UDI registration (**Article 27**). - Post-market surveillance (PMS) and vigilance (**Article 83**-87). - Clinical evaluation (**Article 61**). - The French hospital (if it uses the system under its own name) may also become a manufacturer if it modifies the system or rebrands it (**Article 2(30)**). #### EU AI Act - Impact of Model Weight Sharing: - German Hospital: - Remains a provider under **Article 3(3)** because it developed the system and shares it (even if only model weights are transferred). - no clear transition to deployer status—the hospital is still the original developer. - French Hospital: - If it uses the system as-is, it is a deployer under **Article 3(4)**. - If it modifies the system (e.g., retrains with local data), it may become a provider under **Article 25** (substantial modification). - Obligations for German Hospital (Provider): - Must ensure the French hospital (deployer) receives instructions for use (**Article 13**). - Must monitor performance and report incidents (**Article 72**, 73). - Must update technical documentation if the French hospital’s use introduces new risks (**Article 11**). - Obligations for French Hospital (Deployer): - Must use the system in accordance with instructions (**Article 26(1)**). - Must assign human oversight (**Article 26(2)**). - Must monitor performance and report risks (**Article 26(5)**). #### Cross-Regulatory Chain - MDR: The German hospital loses the **Article 5(5)** exemption and must comply with full MDR obligations. - AI Act: - The German hospital remains a provider (no status change). - The French hospital is a deployer (unless it modifies the system). - Conformity assessment under MDR now integrates AI Act requirements (**Article 43(3)**). --- ### Synthesis Table | Stage | MDR 2017/745 | EU AI Act | Key Trigger | |-------------------------|------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------| | 1. Initial Development | Manufacturer (exempt under **Article 5(5)**) | Provider (**Article 3(3)**) | In-house development & use | | 2. Continuous Learning | Exemption remains (if non-substantial) | Provider (high-risk obligations continue) | Pre-defined retraining logic | | 3. Model Weights Shared | Exemption lost (transfer to another legal entity) → Full MDR compliance | German hospital: Provider (**Article 3(3)**)
French hospital: Deployer (**Article 3(4)**) | Transfer of model weights to French hospital | --- ### Bottom-Line Conclusions 1. MDR Status Transitions: - The German hospital starts as an exempt manufacturer under **Article 5(5)**. - Loses the exemption when it transfers model weights to the French hospital (violates **Article 5(5)**(a)). - Must then comply with full MDR obligations (CE marking, notified body assessment, PMS, etc.). 2. AI Act Status Transitions: - The German hospital remains a provider (**Article 3(3)**) at all stages (no clear transition to deployer). - The French hospital becomes a deployer (**Article 3(4)**) unless it substantially modifies the system (then it becomes a provider under **Article 25**). 3. Key Compliance Risks: - MDR: The **Article 5(5)** exemption is fragile—any transfer (even of model weights) is likely to trigger full MDR compliance. - AI Act: The German hospital cannot escape provider obligations—even internal use does not exempt it from high-risk requirements. - Cross-Regulation: The MDR conformity assessment must integrate AI Act requirements (**Article 43(3)**). 4. Unresolved Questions: - Substantial Modification: If the French hospital retrains the model with local data, does this constitute a substantial modification under **Article 25** AI Act (triggering provider status)? - Intended Purpose: If the French hospital uses the system for a new clinical application, does this change the MDR classification or AI Act risk level? - Data Protection: Does the sharing of model weights (which may contain patient-derived patterns) trigger GDPR obligations (e.g., data subject rights, joint controllership)? Recommendation: The German hospital should conduct a gap analysis to ensure: - MDR compliance (if the exemption is lost). - AI Act compliance (provider obligations for high-risk systems). - GDPR compliance (if patient data is embedded in model weights).
results.judge_text:
1. Final score: 7/10

2. Reliance judgment:
Cannot be relied on without major revision

3. Executive rationale:
The answer is analytically disciplined and cites the correct provisions, but it contains a **fatal backbone error** in the initial actor-status analysis under the AI Act. It misclassifies the German hospital as a "deployer" in the warning flag, then later corrects itself to "provider" without resolving the contradiction. This distortion propagates through the entire analysis, creating material uncertainty about the primary legal route. While the MDR analysis is sound, the AI Act framing is legally unstable and would require substantial revision before a compliance officer could rely on it.

4. Strengths:
- Correctly identifies the MDR Article 5(5) exemption and its fragility when model weights are shared.
- Accurately sequences the analysis (initial status → classification → trigger event → legal effect).
- Uses decisive authority (Article 3(3) AI Act, Article 5(5) MDR) and avoids over-reliance on secondary provisions.
- Explicitly flags unresolved questions (substantial modification, GDPR) that would require escalation.
- Citation verification confirms all quotes are grounded in the legal corpus.

5. Main weaknesses:
1. **Fatal**: Initial actor-status misclassification under the AI Act (warning flag contradicts the body text). The answer oscillates between "deployer" and "provider" for the German hospital, which is a material error under Article 3(3) AI Act.
2. **Major**: Omission of **Article 25 AI Act** (substantial modification) as the primary legal route for the French hospital’s potential transition to provider status. The answer mentions it only as an unresolved question, not as a decisive trigger.
3. **Moderate**: Overemphasis on the MDR exemption’s fragility while understating the AI Act’s **Article 43(3)** integration requirements. The cross-regulatory chain could mislead a reader into prioritizing MDR over AI Act compliance.
4. **Minor**: The "unresolved questions" section could be more actionable (e.g., suggesting specific GDPR Article 26 joint controllership assessments).

6. Senior reviewer test:
- Did the answer identify the correct initial legal status of the relevant actors?
  **No**. The warning flag incorrectly suggests the German hospital might be a "deployer," and the body text does not fully resolve this.
- Did the answer identify the correct primary legal route?
  **Partially**. The MDR route is correct, but the AI Act route is unstable due to the actor-status error and omission of Article 25.
- Would I be comfortable circulating this internally without substantial legal revision?
  **No**. The backbone error and unresolved contradictions would require major restructuring before reliance.

7. What would raise the score by at least 1 full point?
- **Fix the initial actor-status analysis**: Remove the warning flag’s "deployer" claim and consistently classify the German hospital as a **provider** under Article 3(3) AI Act from inception.
- **Anchor the French hospital’s status to Article 25 AI Act**: Explicitly analyze whether sharing model weights + local retraining constitutes a "substantial modification" triggering provider status, rather than treating it as an unresolved question.
- **Clarify the cross-regulatory chain**: Emphasize that AI Act obligations (e.g., Article 43(3)) apply **regardless** of the MDR exemption, and that the MDR conformity assessment must integrate AI Act requirements.

results.label: Cross-regulation — data protection obligations for connected medical device manufacturers (MDR + GDPR)
results.reliance: Can be used as a strong first draft
results.answer:
### 1. Actor Status
Under GDPR, the manufacturer of a connected medical device is a controller (**Article 4(7)** GDPR) because it determines the purposes and means of processing personal data (e.g., patient health data collected by the device). If the manufacturer processes data on behalf of another entity (e.g., a healthcare provider), it may act as a processor, but this is less common for device manufacturers.

Under MDR, the manufacturer is the manufacturer as defined in **Article 2(30)**, responsible for compliance with all MDR obligations, including those related to data generated by the device.

---

### 2. Classification Route
#### GDPR
- The data processed by connected medical devices typically qualifies as special categories of personal data (health data) under **Article 9(1)** GDPR, as it relates to the physical or mental health of individuals (**Article 4(15)** GDPR).
- Processing of such data is prohibited unless a derogation under **Article 9(2)** applies (e.g., **Article 9(2)**(h) for medical diagnosis or **Article 9(2)**(i) for public health).

#### MDR
- The device itself must be classified under **Annex VIII** (e.g., Class IIa, IIb, or III for software with a medical purpose). The classification determines the stringency of conformity assessment and post-market obligations but does not directly affect GDPR obligations.

---

### 3. Trigger Event
- GDPR: Processing of health data (e.g., collection, storage, or transmission of patient data by the device).
- MDR: Placing the device on the market (**Article 10(1)**), which includes ensuring compliance with data-related safety and performance requirements (e.g., **Annex I**, Section 17.2 on data protection).

---

### 4. Obligation Clusters
#### GDPR Obligations (Binding)
1. Lawful Basis for Processing (**Article 6(1)**):
 - A lawful basis (e.g., **Article 6(1)**(c) for legal obligation or **Article 6(1)**(e) for public interest) is required for all processing.
 - For health data, an additional derogation under **Article 9(2)** is required (e.g., **Article 9(2)**(h) for medical diagnosis or **Article 9(2)**(i) for public health).

2. Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) (**Article 35(3)**(b)):
 - Mandatory for large-scale processing of health data (e.g., connected devices used across multiple healthcare providers).
 - Must include:
 - A systematic description of processing operations (**Article 35(7)**(a)).
 - An assessment of necessity and proportionality (**Article 35(7)**(b)).
 - Measures to address risks (**Article 35(7)**(d)).

3. Data Protection by Design and Default (**Article 25**):
 - The device must incorporate data protection principles (e.g., pseudonymisation, minimisation) from the design stage.

4. Security of Processing (**Article 32**):
 - Appropriate technical and organisational measures (e.g., encryption, access controls) to ensure data security.

5. Data Subject Rights (**Articles 12**–22):
 - Manufacturers must facilitate rights such as access (**Article 15**), rectification (**Article 16**), and erasure (**Article 17**).

6. Data Breach Notification (**Article 33**):
 - Notify the supervisory authority within 72 hours of becoming aware of a breach, unless the risk to individuals is low.

7. Records of Processing Activities (**Article 30**):
 - Maintain records of processing activities, including purposes, categories of data, and recipients.

---

#### MDR Obligations (Binding)
1. General Safety and Performance Requirements (**Annex I**):
 - Section 17.2: Devices must be designed to ensure data protection and security, including protection against unauthorised access.
 - Section 17.4: Devices must ensure the integrity and confidentiality of data (e.g., encryption, secure transmission).

2. Technical Documentation (**Article 10(4)** and **Annex II**/III):
 - Must include a risk management file addressing data protection risks (e.g., unauthorised access, data breaches).
 - Must describe measures to ensure data security (e.g., encryption, access controls).

3. Post-Market Surveillance (PMS) (**Article 83**):
 - Manufacturers must monitor the performance and safety of the device, including data-related incidents (e.g., cybersecurity breaches).
 - Data from PMS must be used to update the benefit-risk determination (**Article 83(3)**(a)).

4. Vigilance Reporting (**Article 87**):
 - Report serious incidents (e.g., data breaches leading to patient harm) to competent authorities within 15 days (or 2 days for serious public health threats).
 - Report field safety corrective actions (e.g., software patches for cybersecurity vulnerabilities).

5. UDI and Registration (**Articles 27**, 29, 31):
 - Assign a Unique Device Identifier (UDI) to the device and register it in Eudamed (including data on the manufacturer and device).

6. Instructions for Use (IFU) (**Article 10(11)** and **Annex I**, Section 23):
 - Provide clear instructions on data protection measures (e.g., how to securely handle patient data).

7. Cybersecurity Requirements:
 - While not explicitly detailed in the MDR, **Annex I**, Section 17.2 and MDCG 2019-16 (non-binding guidance) require manufacturers to address cybersecurity risks, including:
 - Secure data transmission.
 - Protection against unauthorised access.
 - Regular software updates to address vulnerabilities.

---

### 5. Cross-Regulatory Dependencies
#### (a) Deferral by Operation of Law
- MDR takes precedence for device safety and performance, including data-related requirements (e.g., **Annex I**, Section 17.2).
- GDPR applies independently for data protection obligations (e.g., lawful basis, DPIA, breach notification).
- No direct deferral between MDR and GDPR, but compliance with MDR’s data security requirements (e.g., encryption) may support GDPR compliance (e.g., **Article 32** security measures).

#### (b) Parallel and Distinct Obligations
| Obligation | MDR | GDPR | Interaction |
|------------------------------|----------------------------------|-----------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Data Security | **Annex I**, Section 17.2 | **Article 32** | MDR’s security requirements (e.g., encryption) support GDPR compliance. |
| Risk Management | **Article 10(2)** + **Annex I**, Section 3 | **Article 35** (DPIA) | MDR’s risk management file may inform the GDPR DPIA, but the DPIA is mandatory. |
| Incident Reporting | **Article 87** (serious incidents) | **Article 33** (data breaches) | A data breach may trigger both MDR (if it causes patient harm) and GDPR. |
| Technical Documentation | **Article 10(4)** + **Annex II**/III | **Article 30** (records of processing)| MDR’s technical file must include data protection measures; GDPR requires records of processing. |

#### (c) Supportive Compliance
- Compliance with MDR’s data security requirements (e.g., encryption, access controls) can support GDPR compliance (e.g., **Article 32** security measures).
- However, MDR compliance does not substitute GDPR obligations (e.g., a DPIA is still required for large-scale health data processing).

---

### 6. Residual Uncertainty
1. Scope of "Health Data":
 - The context does not specify whether the device processes identifiable health data. If data is anonymised, GDPR may not apply, but MDR’s safety requirements (e.g., **Annex I**, Section 17.2) still do.

2. DPIA Triggers:
 - The threshold for "large-scale processing" under **Article 35(3)**(b) is not defined in the context. Guidance from supervisory authorities (e.g., EDPB) may be needed.

3. Cybersecurity Standards:
 - MDR does not prescribe specific cybersecurity standards. Manufacturers must rely on state-of-the-art measures (e.g., ISO 27001, IEC 62443) to comply with **Annex I**, Section 17.2.

4. Interaction with AI Act (if applicable):
 - If the connected device incorporates AI, additional obligations under the EU AI Act may apply (e.g., **Article 9** risk management for high-risk AI systems). This is outside the scope of the current analysis.

---

### 7. Interpretive Guidance (Non-Binding)
The MDCG 2019-16 guidance (not provided in the context but referenced in regulatory practice) interprets **Annex I**, Section 17.2 of the MDR as requiring:
- Data protection by design: Incorporate privacy and security measures from the outset.
- Secure data transmission: Use encryption for data in transit and at rest.
- Access controls: Implement authentication and authorisation mechanisms.
- Regular updates: Patch vulnerabilities to maintain data security.

This guidance is non-binding but provides a practical framework for compliance. Manufacturers should align their practices with these recommendations while ensuring they meet the binding requirements of both MDR and GDPR.
results.judge_text:
1. Final score: 8.5/10

2. Reliance judgment:
**Can be used as a strong first draft**

3. Executive rationale:
The answer is legally sound in its backbone, correctly identifying the manufacturer as a GDPR controller and MDR manufacturer, and mapping the primary legal routes (GDPR Article 9 for health data, MDR Annex I for safety/performance). It sequences issues logically and anchors conclusions in decisive provisions. While minor refinements are needed (e.g., clearer DPIA triggers, explicit handling of anonymised data), it would require only targeted expert review rather than major restructuring for internal compliance use.

4. Strengths:
- **Correct legal backbone**: Accurately identifies the manufacturer as a GDPR controller (Article 4(7)) and MDR manufacturer (Article 2(30)), and correctly triggers GDPR via Article 9(1) health data processing.
- **Decisive authority**: Relies on primary provisions (e.g., GDPR Articles 6, 9, 32, 35; MDR Annex I, Article 87) without omitting critical anchors.
- **Analytical discipline**: Follows the required order (status → classification → trigger → obligations → uncertainty) without jumping to conclusions.
- **Practical utility**: Provides actionable clusters (e.g., DPIA requirements, breach notification timelines) and a clear cross-regulatory table.
- **Calibration**: Explicitly flags residual uncertainties (e.g., "large-scale" thresholds, anonymisation) without overclaiming.

5. Main weaknesses:
- **Moderate**: **DPIA triggers**: While Article 35(3)(b) is cited, the answer could specify that "large-scale" processing of health data *always* requires a DPIA under EDPB guidance (e.g., Guidelines 3/2018), not just "may" require one. This risks undercompliance.
- **Moderate**: **Anonymised data**: The answer notes GDPR may not apply to anonymised data but does not clarify that MDR’s safety requirements (e.g., Annex I, Section 17.2) still apply *even if data is anonymised*, which could mislead on residual obligations.
- **Minor**: **Cybersecurity standards**: The answer correctly states MDR lacks prescriptive standards but could name specific harmonised standards (e.g., ISO 27001, IEC 62443) as *de facto* requirements under "state-of-the-art" principles.
- **Minor**: **AI Act interaction**: The disclaimer is appropriate, but the answer could briefly note that AI-enabled devices may trigger *additional* GDPR obligations (e.g., Article 22 automated decision-making) beyond the scope of the question.

6. Senior reviewer test:
- **Correct initial legal status?** Yes. Manufacturer is correctly identified as a GDPR controller and MDR manufacturer.
- **Correct primary legal route?** Yes. GDPR obligations are anchored in Article 9(1) health data processing, and MDR obligations in Annex I safety/performance requirements.
- **Comfortable circulating internally?** Yes, with *targeted* legal review (e.g., to tighten DPIA triggers and anonymisation caveats). No major restructuring needed.

7. What would raise the score by at least 1 full point?
- **Clarify DPIA triggers**: Explicitly state that processing health data at scale *always* requires a DPIA under EDPB guidance, not just "may" require one.
- **Address anonymised data**: Specify that MDR’s safety requirements (e.g., Annex I, Section 17.2) apply *regardless* of whether data is anonymised, to avoid undercompliance.
- **Name cybersecurity standards**: Reference harmonised standards (e.g., ISO 27001) as *de facto* requirements under "state-of-the-art" principles in MDR Annex I.
- **Tighten AI Act caveat**: Note that AI-enabled devices may trigger *additional* GDPR obligations (e.g., Article 22) even if the AI Act is out of scope.

results.label: Benchmark — Class IIb SaMD with continuous learning, PROVIDER obligations (AI Act + MDR)
results.reliance: Can be used as a strong first draft
results.answer:
> ASSESSMENT STATUS — Provisional legal qualification assessment. This answer should be read as a case-specific compliance analysis, not as an automatic status determination.

### 1. Actor Status
#### Under the EU AI Act
Your company is the provider of the AI system under **Article 3(3)** of the AI Act because you developed the system and placed it on the market under your own name or trademark. This status applies regardless of whether the system is deployed internally or externally.

#### Under the MDR 2017/745
You are the manufacturer of the medical device under **Article 2(1)** of the MDR, as you are the entity responsible for its design, development, and placing on the market.

---

### 2. Classification Route
#### AI Act High-Risk Classification
Your AI system qualifies as high-risk under **Article 6(1)** of the AI Act via Route I (**Annex I**) because:
- It is software as a medical device (SaMD) covered by Regulation (EU) 2017/745 (MDR), which is listed in **Annex I**, Section A, point 11 of the AI Act.
- As a Class IIb medical device, it is subject to third-party conformity assessment under the MDR (required for Class IIa, IIb, and III devices), satisfying **Article 6(1)**(b).

No derogation applies to Route I, so the system is automatically high-risk under the AI Act.

#### MDR Classification
Your device is already classified as Class IIb under **Annex VIII** of the MDR, which determines its conformity assessment route under the MDR.

---

### 3. Trigger Event
The obligations under both regulations are triggered by:
- Placing the device on the market (first making available in the EU for commercial use) under **Article 2(27)** MDR and **Article 3(10)** AI Act.
- Putting the device into service (first use by the final user) under **Article 2(28)** MDR and **Article 3(11)** AI Act.

For continuous-learning models, additional obligations apply throughout the lifecycle, particularly in post-market surveillance and monitoring.

---

### 4. Legal Effects (Obligations)
#### AI Act Obligations for High-Risk AI Systems
As the provider of a high-risk AI system, you must comply with the following **Article 16** obligations:
- Conformity with Section 2 requirements (**Article 16**(a)), including:
 - Risk management system (**Article 9**).
 - Data governance (**Article 10**), ensuring training/validation data are relevant, representative, and free from bias.
 - Technical documentation (**Article 11**), including design specifications, risk management, and post-market monitoring plans.
 - Record-keeping (automated logs) (**Article 12**), ensuring traceability of system operations.
 - Accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity (**Article 15**), with declared performance metrics in the instructions for use.
 - Human oversight (**Article 14**), ensuring deployers can properly monitor and intervene.
 - Transparency and provision of information (**Article 13**), including instructions for use and technical documentation for deployers.
- Quality management system (QMS) (**Article 17**), covering compliance, design control, data management, post-market monitoring, and incident reporting.
- Conformity assessment (**Article 43(3)**):
 - Since your device is a Class IIb medical device, the MDR conformity assessment procedure applies, and the AI Act requirements (Section 2 of Chapter III) are integrated into this assessment.
 - The notified body under the MDR will also assess compliance with the AI Act requirements.
- EU declaration of conformity (**Article 47**), which must reference both the MDR and AI Act.
- CE marking (**Article 48**), affixed to the device or its documentation.
- Registration in the EU database (**Article 49(1)**), before placing the system on the market.
- Post-market monitoring (PMS) (**Article 72**), including continuous monitoring of performance, accuracy, and safety, with a post-market monitoring plan.
- Serious incident reporting (**Article 73**), including reporting to the relevant authorities under both the MDR and AI Act.
- Corrective actions (**Article 20**), including recalls, withdrawals, or updates if non-conformities are identified.
- Cooperation with competent authorities (**Article 21**), providing documentation and logs upon request.

#### MDR Obligations for Class IIb Medical Devices
As the manufacturer of a Class IIb medical device, you must comply with:
- General obligations (**Article 10** MDR), including:
 - Risk management (**Annex I**, Chapter I).
 - Clinical evaluation (**Article 61** MDR), demonstrating conformity with safety and performance requirements.
 - Technical documentation (**Annex II** and III MDR).
 - Post-market surveillance (PMS) (**Article 83** MDR), including a PMS plan and periodic safety update reports (PSURs).
 - Vigilance reporting (**Article 87** MDR), including serious incident reporting to competent authorities.
 - Unique Device Identification (UDI) (**Article 27** MDR).
- Conformity assessment (**Annex IX** or X MDR), involving a notified body for Class IIb devices.
- EU declaration of conformity (**Annex IV** MDR).
- CE marking (**Article 20** MDR).

#### Continuous-Learning Specific Obligations
For continuous-learning models, the following obligations apply under both regulations:
- AI Act:
 - **Article 15(4)** requires that high-risk AI systems that continue to learn after being placed on the market must:
 - Eliminate or reduce the risk of biased outputs influencing future operations (feedback loops).
 - Implement appropriate mitigation measures to address feedback loops.
 - **Article 72** (Post-market monitoring) requires continuous monitoring of system performance, including:
 - Performance drift detection.
 - Bias and robustness monitoring.
 - Cybersecurity monitoring.
 - **Article 9** (Risk management system) must account for risks associated with continuous learning, including:
 - Unintended model behaviour.
 - Data drift.
 - Performance degradation.
- MDR:
 - **Article 83** (Post-market surveillance) requires monitoring of the device’s performance throughout its lifecycle, including:
 - Performance monitoring (e.g., accuracy, sensitivity, specificity).
 - Adverse event detection.
 - **Article 87** (Vigilance) requires reporting of serious incidents, including those caused by model updates or performance degradation.
 - **Annex I**, Chapter I (General Safety and Performance Requirements) requires that:
 - The device remains safe and effective throughout its lifecycle.
 - Any modifications (including model updates) are assessed for significant changes under **Article 120** MDR and may require a new conformity assessment.

---

### 5. Cross-Regulatory Chain
The AI Act and MDR obligations are complementary and must be integrated:
- Conformity assessment:
 - The MDR conformity assessment procedure applies (**Article 43(3)** AI Act), and the notified body under the MDR will also assess compliance with the AI Act requirements (Section 2 of Chapter III).
 - A single EU declaration of conformity can be used to cover both regulations (**Article 47(3)** AI Act).
- Post-market monitoring and vigilance:
 - The PMS system under the MDR can be used to fulfil the post-market monitoring requirements under the AI Act (**Article 72**).
 - Serious incident reporting must comply with both **Article 87** MDR and **Article 73** AI Act, with reports submitted to the relevant authorities under both frameworks.
- Data governance and bias mitigation:
 - The AI Act’s data governance requirements (**Article 10**) must be integrated into the MDR’s clinical evaluation and risk management processes.
 - Bias and robustness monitoring under the AI Act must be documented in the MDR’s technical documentation and PMS reports.
- Continuous learning and model updates:
 - Any substantial modifications to the device (including model updates) must be assessed under **Article 120** MDR to determine if a new conformity assessment is required.
 - The AI Act’s risk management system (**Article 9**) must account for risks associated with continuous learning, and these risks must be documented in the MDR’s risk management file (**Annex I**, Chapter I).

---

### 6. Residual Uncertainty
The following aspects may require case-by-case assessment:
1. Definition of "substantial modification" under the MDR:
 - The MDR does not provide a bright-line rule for what is likely to constitute a substantial modification for continuous-learning models. MDCG guidance (e.g., MDCG 2020-3) provides examples, but the assessment depends on the nature and impact of the model update.
 - If a model update is deemed substantial, a new conformity assessment may be required under the MDR, which would also trigger a reassessment of AI Act compliance.

2. Integration of AI Act and MDR PMS systems:
 - While the PMS systems under both regulations are complementary, the frequency and depth of monitoring for continuous-learning models may require additional documentation and reporting beyond standard MDR requirements.
 - The AI Act’s post-market monitoring plan (**Article 72**) may need to include specific metrics for performance drift, bias, and robustness, which are not explicitly required under the MDR.

3. Human oversight requirements:
 - The AI Act’s human oversight requirements (**Article 14**) must be implemented in a way that aligns with the MDR’s usability and clinical evaluation requirements.
 - The level of human intervention required for continuous-learning models may vary depending on the risk profile of the device and the impact of model updates.

4. Cybersecurity risks:
 - The AI Act’s cybersecurity requirements (**Article 15(5)**) must be integrated with the MDR’s cybersecurity requirements (**Annex I**, Chapter II, Section 17).
 - Continuous-learning models may introduce additional cybersecurity risks (e.g., adversarial attacks on model updates), which must be addressed in the risk management file under both regulations.

---

### Conclusion
Your Class IIb SaMD with continuous-learning capabilities is subject to:
1. High-risk obligations under the AI Act via Route I (**Article 6(1)** + **Annex I**), including:
 - Conformity assessment integrated with the MDR procedure.
 - Risk management, data governance, technical documentation, and post-market monitoring tailored to continuous-learning risks.
 - Specific requirements for feedback loops, performance drift, and bias mitigation.
2. Class IIb obligations under the MDR, including:
 - Conformity assessment involving a notified body.
 - Clinical evaluation, PMS, vigilance, and risk management throughout the lifecycle.
 - Assessment of substantial modifications for model updates.

Key actions for compliance:
- Integrate AI Act requirements into your MDR conformity assessment (e.g., technical documentation, risk management, PMS).
- Implement a post-market monitoring system that addresses performance drift, bias, and cybersecurity risks for continuous-learning models.
- Document all model updates and assess whether they constitute substantial modifications under the MDR.
- Ensure human oversight mechanisms are in place to monitor and intervene in the continuous-learning process.
- Align serious incident reporting under both the MDR and AI Act.

The AI Act and MDR are designed to work together, and compliance with one regulation can support compliance with the other. However, continuous-learning models introduce additional complexity, particularly in post-market monitoring and modification management, which may require tailored processes and documentation.

results.label: AI Act high-risk qualification — triage chatbot (Annex III vs Article 6(1))
results.reliance: Can be relied on with minimal revision
results.answer:
> ASSESSMENT STATUS — Provisional legal qualification assessment. This answer should be read as a case-specific compliance analysis, not as an automatic status determination.

### Regulatory Analysis: High-Risk Classification of the AI Chatbot Under the EU AI Act

#### 1. Actor Status
The company is the provider of the AI system under **Article 3(3)** of the AI Act, as it developed the system and placed it on the market or put it into service under its own name or trademark.

#### 2. Classification Route
The AI Act establishes two routes for high-risk classification, which must be analysed in order:
- Route I (**Article 6(1)** + **Annex I**): Applies if the AI system is a safety component of a product (or is itself a product) covered by Union harmonisation legislation listed in **Annex I**, and the product requires third-party conformity assessment under that legislation.
- Route II (**Article 6(2)** + **Annex III**): Applies if the AI system falls within one of the 8 use-case categories in **Annex III**, subject to the **Article 6(3)** derogation for narrow procedural tasks (unless profiling of natural persons is involved).

For medical-device AI systems, Route I is the primary route and must be analysed first.

---

### 3. Route I Analysis (Article 6(1) + Annex I)
#### 3.1. Is the AI System a Medical Device Under the MDR?
The AI chatbot is software intended to provide information used to take decisions with diagnostic or therapeutic purposes (triage and recommendation of emergency care). Under **Annex VIII**, Chapter III, point 6.3 (Rule 11) of the MDR, such software is classified as:
- Class IIa if it provides information for decisions with diagnosis/therapeutic purposes (general case),
- Class IIb if the decisions may cause a serious deterioration of a person’s state of health or a surgical intervention, or
- Class III if the decisions may cause death or an irreversible deterioration of a person’s state of health.

Likely classification: The chatbot’s recommendations (e.g., "seek emergency care") could lead to serious deterioration of health if incorrect, suggesting Class IIb is the most probable classification. However, this depends on the specific intended purpose and risk profile of the software, which must be assessed case-by-case.

#### 3.2. Is the AI System a Safety Component of a Medical Device?
The chatbot is itself a medical device (software as a medical device, SaMD) under the MDR. Under **Article 6(1)**(a), an AI system that is itself a product covered by Union harmonisation legislation (e.g., the MDR) qualifies for Route I.

#### 3.3. Does the Medical Device Require Third-Party Conformity Assessment?
- Class IIa, IIb, and III medical devices require third-party conformity assessment by a notified body under the MDR.
- If the chatbot is classified as Class IIb, it satisfies **Article 6(1)**(b).

#### 3.4. Conclusion for Route I
If the chatbot is classified as Class IIa, IIb, or III under the MDR, it is high-risk under **Article 6(1)** + **Annex I** of the AI Act. No derogation applies to Route I.

---

### 4. Route II Analysis (Annex III)
#### 4.1. Does the AI System Fall Under Any Annex III Category?Annex III lists 8 use-case categories, none of which explicitly mention medical diagnosis or triage. The closest categories are:
- **Annex III(5)**(a): AI systems intended to be used by public authorities or on their behalf to evaluate the eligibility of natural persons for essential public assistance benefits and services, including healthcare services.
 - Applicability: The chatbot recommends whether to seek emergency care but does not evaluate eligibility for healthcare services (e.g., insurance coverage, treatment approval). This category is unlikely to apply.
- **Annex III(5)**(d): AI systems intended to evaluate and classify emergency calls or to dispatch emergency first response services.
 - Applicability: The chatbot triages symptoms but does not evaluate emergency calls (e.g., 112/911 calls) or dispatch first responders. This category is unlikely to apply.

#### 4.2. Article 6(3) Derogation (Narrow Procedural Tasks)
Even if the chatbot were to fall under **Annex III(5)**(a) or (5)(d), **Article 6(3)** allows a derogation if the system:
- Performs a narrow procedural task,
- Improves the result of a previously completed human activity,
- Detects decision-making patterns without replacing human assessment, or
- Performs a preparatory task to an assessment relevant to **Annex III**.

However, profiling of natural persons (e.g., analysing patient symptoms to predict health risks) always remains high-risk under **Article 6(3)**, subparagraph 3.

Likely outcome: The chatbot profiles patients (analysing symptoms to recommend care), so the **Article 6(3)** derogation does not apply. However, Route II is secondary to Route I, and since Route I already applies, this is academic.

---

### 5. Trigger Event
The chatbot is placed on the market or put into service in the EU, triggering the high-risk obligations under the AI Act.

---

### 6. Legal Effects
#### 6.1. AI Act Obligations (High-Risk AI System)
If the chatbot is high-risk under **Article 6(1)** + **Annex I**, the provider must comply with Chapter III, Section 2 of the AI Act, including:
- Risk management system (**Article 9**),
- Data governance (**Article 10**),
- Technical documentation (**Article 11**),
- Record-keeping (**Article 12**),
- Transparency and human oversight (**Article 13**),
- Accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity (**Article 15**),
- Conformity assessment (**Article 43(3)**): The MDR conformity assessment procedure applies, with AI Act requirements integrated (e.g., **Annex VII**, points 4.3–4.6 for technical documentation and post-market monitoring).
- Registration in the EU database (**Article 49**),
- Post-market monitoring (**Article 72**),
- Serious incident reporting (**Article 73**).

#### 6.2. MDR Obligations (Medical Device)
The chatbot must comply with the MDR as a Class IIa, IIb, or III medical device, including:
- General safety and performance requirements (**Annex I**),
- Clinical evaluation (demonstrating safety, performance, and clinical benefit),
- Technical documentation (**Annex II** and III),
- UDI registration (**Article 27**),
- Post-market surveillance (**Article 83**–86),
- Vigilance reporting (**Article 87**–89).

#### 6.3. Cross-Regulatory Dependencies
- Conformity assessment: The MDR conformity assessment procedure applies, with AI Act requirements integrated (**Article 43(3)**).
- Post-market monitoring: The MDR post-market surveillance system must align with the AI Act post-market monitoring requirements (**Article 72**).
- Clinical evidence: The MDR clinical evaluation must address AI-specific risks (e.g., bias, robustness, adaptiveness) as required by the AI Act (**Article 10(5)**).
- Data protection: If the chatbot processes health data, it must comply with GDPR **Article 9** (processing of special categories of personal data). The AI Act’s data governance requirements (**Article 10**) must align with GDPR.

---

### 7. Residual Uncertainty
#### 7.1. MDR Classification of the Chatbot
The MDR classification (Class IIa, IIb, or III) depends on the specific intended purpose and risk profile of the chatbot. If the chatbot’s recommendations could lead to serious deterioration of health (e.g., delayed treatment due to incorrect triage), Class IIb is likely. However, a case-by-case assessment is required.

#### 7.2. Interaction with Annex III
If the chatbot were to evaluate eligibility for healthcare services (e.g., insurance approval) or dispatch emergency services, it could fall under **Annex III(5)**(a) or (5)(d). However, the current description (symptom triage and recommendation) does not clearly fit these categories.

#### 7.3. What Would Change the Conclusion?
- If the chatbot is classified as Class I under the MDR: It would not be high-risk under Route I, but Route II would still need to be assessed (unlikely to apply).
- If the chatbot’s intended purpose changes (e.g., to evaluate eligibility for healthcare services or dispatch emergency services): It could become high-risk under **Annex III(5)**(a) or (5)(d).
- If the chatbot does not profile patients: The **Article 6(3)** derogation could apply, but this is unlikely given the nature of symptom triage.

---

### 8. Conclusion
The AI chatbot is likely a high-risk AI system under **Article 6(1)** + **Annex I** of the AI Act, because:
1. It is software as a medical device (SaMD) under the MDR, likely classified as Class IIb (due to the risk of serious deterioration of health from incorrect triage).
2. It is itself a product covered by the MDR (**Annex I**, point 11).
3. Class IIb medical devices require third-party conformity assessment, satisfying **Article 6(1)**(b).

Route II (**Annex III**) does not apply to the current use case (symptom triage and recommendation), and even if it did, Route I takes precedence.

#### Key Obligations
- AI Act: Full high-risk AI system obligations (risk management, data governance, technical documentation, post-market monitoring, etc.), with MDR conformity assessment as the primary procedure.
- MDR: Full medical device obligations (clinical evaluation, post-market surveillance, vigilance reporting, etc.).
- GDPR: Compliance with **Article 9** (processing of health data) if applicable.

#### Next Steps for Compliance
1. Confirm MDR classification of the chatbot (likely Class IIb).
2. Integrate AI Act requirements into the MDR conformity assessment procedure.
3. Align post-market surveillance under the MDR with AI Act post-market monitoring.
4. Ensure GDPR compliance for health data processing.

3. Executive rationale:
The answer is legally robust, correctly identifies the primary legal route (Article 6(1) + Annex I via MDR classification), and anchors its analysis in decisive provisions. It sequences issues logically, handles uncertainty transparently, and provides actionable compliance guidance. results.label: AI Act GPAI model provider — systemic-risk obligations (Article 53 + 55)
results.reliance: Can be relied on with minimal revision
results.answer:
Under the EU AI Act, a provider of a general-purpose AI model with systemic risk is subject to a comprehensive set of obligations that build upon the baseline requirements for all general-purpose AI models and introduce additional, stricter measures to address systemic risks. These obligations are primarily governed by **Article 55** (specific to systemic-risk models), **Article 53** (general obligations for all general-purpose AI models), and **Article 54** (obligations related to authorised representatives). Below is a structured analysis of these obligations, grounded exclusively in the EU AI Act provisions provided in the regulatory context.

---

### 1. Baseline Obligations (Article 53) – Applicable to All General-Purpose AI Models
Before addressing systemic-risk-specific obligations, providers of general-purpose AI models (including those with systemic risk) must comply with the baseline requirements set out in **Article 53(1)**. These include:

#### a. Technical Documentation (Article 53(1)(a))
- The provider must draw up and keep up-to-date technical documentation of the model, including:
 - Its training and testing process,
 - The results of its evaluation,
 - The minimum information set out in **Annex XI** (e.g., model architecture, training data sources, performance metrics, and risk mitigation measures).
- This documentation must be provided to the AI Office and national competent authorities upon request.

#### b. Information for Downstream Providers (Article 53(1)(b))
- The provider must draw up, keep up-to-date, and make available information and documentation to providers of AI systems who intend to integrate the general-purpose AI model into their systems.
- This information must:
 - Enable downstream providers to understand the model’s capabilities and limitations and comply with their own obligations under the AI Act (e.g., high-risk AI system requirements).
 - Include, at a minimum, the elements set out in **Annex XII** (e.g., intended use, performance characteristics, known limitations, and integration guidelines).
- Confidentiality safeguards: The provider may withhold information protected by intellectual property rights, confidential business information, or trade secrets, provided this does not hinder compliance with the AI Act.

#### c. Copyright Compliance (Article 53(1)(c))
- The provider must put in place a policy to comply with Union law on copyright and related rights, specifically:
 - Identifying and complying with reservations of rights expressed under **Article 4(3)** of Directive (EU) 2019/790 (e.g., opt-out mechanisms for text and data mining).
 - Using state-of-the-art technologies to ensure compliance.

#### d. Public Summary of Training Data (Article 53(1)(d))
- The provider must draw up and make publicly available a sufficiently detailed summary of the content used for training the model, following a template provided by the AI Office.
- The summary must be clear and accessible but may exclude confidential or proprietary information.

#### Exemption for Open-Source Models (Article 53(2))
- The obligations under **Article 53(1)**(a) and (b) (technical documentation and information for downstream providers) do not apply to providers of general-purpose AI models that:
 - Are released under a free and open-source licence,
 - Allow access, usage, modification, and distribution of the model, and
 - Make parameters (including weights), model architecture, and usage information publicly available.
- Exception: This exemption does not apply to general-purpose AI models with systemic risk, even if they are open-source.

---

### 2. Systemic-Risk-Specific Obligations (Article 55)
Providers of general-purpose AI models with systemic risk must comply with additional obligations under **Article 55(1)**, which are cumulative to those in **Article 53**. These obligations are designed to mitigate the broader societal and safety risks posed by such models.

#### a. Model Evaluation and Adversarial Testing (Article 55(1)(a))
- The provider must perform model evaluation in accordance with:
 - Standardised protocols and tools reflecting the state of the art,
 - Adversarial testing to identify and mitigate systemic risks (e.g., robustness against malicious inputs, bias amplification, or unintended harmful outputs).
- The evaluation must be documented and made available to authorities upon request.

#### b. Systemic Risk Assessment and Mitigation (Article 55(1)(b))
- The provider must:
 - Assess possible systemic risks at the Union level, including their sources (e.g., training data biases, model architecture flaws, or deployment contexts).
 - Mitigate these risks through appropriate measures (e.g., model adjustments, deployment restrictions, or monitoring mechanisms).
- The assessment must consider risks stemming from:
 - The development of the model,
 - Its placing on the market, or
 - Its use in downstream applications.

#### c. Incident Reporting (Article 55(1)(c))
- The provider must:
 - Keep track of and document relevant information about serious incidents (as defined in **Article 3(45)**) and possible corrective measures.
 - Report such incidents without undue delay to:
 - The AI Office, and
 - National competent authorities, where appropriate.
- Serious incidents include events where the model’s output directly or indirectly leads to harm (e.g., safety risks, fundamental rights violations, or societal disruptions).

#### d. Cybersecurity Protection (Article 55(1)(d))
- The provider must ensure an adequate level of cybersecurity protection for:
 - The general-purpose AI model itself, and
 - The physical infrastructure supporting the model (e.g., servers, data centres).
- This includes measures to prevent unauthorised access, data breaches, or model tampering.

#### Compliance Mechanisms (Article 55(2))
- Providers may demonstrate compliance with **Article 55(1)** obligations through:
 - Codes of practice (under **Article 56**), until a harmonised standard is published.
 - European harmonised standards, which grant a presumption of conformity.
 - Alternative adequate means of compliance, if the provider does not adhere to a code of practice or harmonised standard. Such alternatives must be assessed by the Commission.

#### Confidentiality (Article 55(3))
- Any information or documentation obtained under **Article 55** (including trade secrets) must be treated in accordance with the confidentiality obligations set out in **Article 78**.

---

### 3. Authorised Representative Obligations (Article 54)
If the provider of a general-purpose AI model with systemic risk is established outside the EU, they must appoint an authorised representative in the Union before placing the model on the market. The authorised representative’s obligations include:

#### a. Verification of Compliance (Article 54(3)(a))
- The authorised representative must verify that:
 - The technical documentation (**Annex XI**) has been drawn up, and
 - The provider has fulfilled all obligations under **Article 53** and **Article 55**.

#### b. Documentation Retention (Article 54(3)(b))
- The authorised representative must keep a copy of the technical documentation and the provider’s contact details for 10 years after the model is placed on the market.
- This documentation must be made available to the AI Office and national competent authorities upon request.

#### c. Information Provision (Article 54(3)(c))
- The authorised representative must provide the AI Office with all information and documentation necessary to demonstrate compliance with Chapter V (general-purpose AI models) upon a reasoned request.

#### d. Cooperation with Authorities (Article 54(3)(d))
- The authorised representative must cooperate with the AI Office and national competent authorities in any action related to the model, including when it is integrated into AI systems placed on the market or put into service in the EU.

#### e. Mandate Termination (Article 54(5))
- The authorised representative must terminate the mandate if it considers or has reason to consider that the provider is acting contrary to its obligations under the AI Act.
- The authorised representative must immediately inform the AI Office of the termination and the reasons therefor.

#### Exemption for Open-Source Models (Article 54(6))
- The obligation to appoint an authorised representative does not apply to providers of general-purpose AI models that:
 - Are released under a free and open-source licence,
 - Allow access, usage, modification, and distribution, and
 - Make parameters, model architecture, and usage information publicly available.
- Exception: This exemption does not apply if the model presents systemic risks.

---

### 4. Procedural Obligations for Systemic Risk Classification (Article 52)
Providers of general-purpose AI models that meet the systemic risk threshold (e.g., training compute > 10²⁵ FLOPs under **Article 51(2)**) must comply with notification and procedural requirements:

#### a. Notification to the Commission (Article 52(1))
- The provider must notify the Commission without delay and within two weeks of:
 - The model meeting the systemic risk condition (e.g., exceeding the compute threshold), or
 - The provider becoming aware that the model will meet the condition.
- The notification must include information demonstrating that the condition has been met.

#### b. Exceptional Arguments (Article 52(2))
- The provider may submit sufficiently substantiated arguments to demonstrate that, despite meeting the systemic risk condition, the model does not present systemic risks due to its specific characteristics.
- The Commission will assess these arguments and may reject them if they are not sufficiently substantiated (**Article 52(3)**).

#### c. Reassessment Request (Article 52(5))
- If the Commission designates the model as having systemic risk (e.g., following a qualified alert from the scientific panel under **Article 90(1)**(a)), the provider may request a reassessment after six months.
- The request must include objective, detailed, and new reasons that have arisen since the designation decision.

#### d. Public List of Systemic-Risk Models (Article 52(6))
- The Commission must publish and maintain a list of general-purpose AI models with systemic risk, while protecting confidential business information and trade secrets.

---

### 5. Enforcement and Supervision (Article 88)
- The AI Office (under the Commission) has exclusive powers to supervise and enforce compliance with Chapter V (general-purpose AI models), including systemic-risk obligations (**Article 88(1)**).
- Market surveillance authorities may request the Commission to exercise these powers if necessary to assist with their tasks (**Article 88(2)**).
- Providers are entitled to procedural rights under **Article 94**, which applies mutatis mutandis the safeguards of **Article 18** of Regulation (EU) 2019/1020.

---

### 6. Key Takeaways for Providers
| Obligation | Applicability | Governing Provision |
|-----------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------|
| Technical documentation | All general-purpose AI models (including systemic-risk models) | **Article 53(1)**(a) |
| Information for downstream providers | All general-purpose AI models (including systemic-risk models) | **Article 53(1)**(b) |
| Copyright compliance policy | All general-purpose AI models (including systemic-risk models) | **Article 53(1)**(c) |
| Public summary of training data | All general-purpose AI models (including systemic-risk models) | **Article 53(1)**(d) |
| Exemption for open-source models | Applies to **Article 53(1)**(a) and (b) only (not systemic-risk models) | **Article 53(2)** |
| Model evaluation & adversarial testing | Systemic-risk models only | **Article 55(1)**(a) |
| Systemic risk assessment & mitigation | Systemic-risk models only | **Article 55(1)**(b) |
| Incident reporting | Systemic-risk models only | **Article 55(1)**(c) |
| Cybersecurity protection | Systemic-risk models only | **Article 55(1)**(d) |
| Authorised representative | Providers established outside the EU (unless open-source exemption applies) | **Article 54** |
| Notification to Commission | Models meeting systemic-risk conditions | **Article 52(1)** |
| Reassessment request | Models designated as systemic-risk | **Article 52(5)** |

---

### 7. Compliance Pathways
Providers of general-purpose AI models with systemic risk may demonstrate compliance through:
1. Codes of practice (under **Article 56**), until harmonised standards are published.
2. Harmonised standards, which grant a presumption of conformity.
3. Alternative adequate means of compliance, if the provider does not adhere to a code of practice or harmonised standard. Such alternatives must be assessed by the Commission.

---

### 8. Non-Compliance Consequences
Failure to comply with these obligations may result in:
- Corrective measures (e.g., model adjustments, withdrawal from the market),
- Fines (up to 3% of global turnover or €15 million, whichever is higher, under **Article 99**),
- Prohibition of the model’s use in the EU, or
- Legal action by national competent authorities or the AI Office.

1. Final score: 9/10

results.label: AI Act prohibited practices — workplace emotion recognition (Article 5)
results.reliance: Cannot be relied on without major revision
results.answer:
Under the EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689), the deployment of an AI system that infers employees' emotions from webcam feeds during work is prohibited in most cases, governed by **Article 5(1)**(f).

### Key Provisions:
1. Prohibition on Emotion Recognition in Workplace (**Article 5(1)**(f)):
 The AI Act explicitly prohibits the placing on the market, putting into service, or use of AI systems to infer emotions of natural persons in the workplace, except where the system is intended for medical or safety reasons. The operative clause states:
 > […]

> the placing on the market, the putting into service for this specific purpose, or the use of AI systems to infer emotions of a natural person in the areas of workplace and education institutions, except where the use of the AI system is intended to be put in place or into the market for medical or safety reasons;

.

 This prohibition applies regardless of whether the system is classified as "high-risk" under **Annex III** or not.

2. Exceptions for Medical or Safety Reasons:
 The prohibition does not apply if the AI system is deployed strictly for medical or safety purposes (e.g., monitoring employee fatigue for safety-critical roles). However, such use cases must still comply with other applicable legal frameworks, including the MDR 2017/745 (if the system qualifies as a medical device) and GDPR (for data processing).

3. Transparency Obligations (**Article 50(3)**):
 Even if an exception applies (e.g., medical/safety use), deployers of emotion recognition systems must:
 - Inform natural persons exposed to the system of its operation.
 - Process personal data in compliance with GDPR (Regulation 2016/679) and other applicable data protection laws.

> 3.   Deployers of an emotion recognition system or a biometric categorisation system shall inform the natural persons exposed thereto of the operation of the system, and shall process the personal data in accordance with Regulations (EU) 2016/679 and (EU) 2018/1725 and Directive (EU) 2016/680, as applicable. […]

.

4. High-Risk Classification (**Annex III**, Point 1(c)):
 Emotion recognition systems are classified as high-risk AI systems under **Annex III**, Point 1(c) if their use is permitted (e.g., for medical/safety reasons). High-risk systems are subject to stringent requirements under Chapter III of the AI Act, including risk management, data governance, and human oversight.

### Regulatory Context:
- The prohibition in **Article 5(1)**(f) is absolute for workplace deployments unless the system is for medical or safety purposes.
- **Recital 44** reinforces this prohibition, citing concerns about the scientific reliability of emotion recognition systems and their potential for discriminatory outcomes or intrusions into fundamental rights

> (44) There are serious concerns about the scientific basis of AI systems aiming to identify or infer emotions, particularly as expression of emotions vary considerably across cultures and situations, and even within a single individual. Among the key shortcomings of such systems are the limited reliability, the lack of specificity and the limited generalisability. […]

.
- **Recital 63** clarifies that the AI Act does not provide a legal basis for processing personal data (e.g., biometric data) unless explicitly stated, meaning compliance with GDPR is mandatory

> (63) The fact that an AI system is classified as a high-risk AI system under this Regulation should not be interpreted as indicating that the use of the system is lawful under other acts of Union law or under national law compatible with Union law, such as on the protection of personal data, on the use of polygraphs and similar tools or other systems to detect the emotional state of natural persons. Any such use should continue to occur solely in accordance with the applicable requirements resulting from the Charter and from the applicable acts of secondary Union law and national law. […]

.

### Conclusion:
Deploying an AI system to infer employees' emotions from webcam feeds during work is unlawful under the EU AI Act unless the system is strictly for medical or safety reasons. The governing provision is **Article 5(1)**(f). Even in permitted cases, transparency obligations (**Article 50(3)**) and high-risk requirements (Chapter III) apply. Compliance with GDPR is also required for any processing of personal data.

> ⚠ **ATTRIBUTION FLAG** — 1 of 1 quote(s) contain real regulatory text that does not belong to the provision they cite (or concatenate text from several provisions under one citation) and have been removed. Re-check which provision each obligation actually comes from.
> - “the placing on the market, the putting into service for this specific purpose, or the use of AI systems to infer emot...”

results.label: AI Act substantial modification — re-conformity of a high-risk system (Article 43(4))
results.reliance: Cannot be relied on without major revision
results.answer:
### Substantial Modification Under the EU AI Act: When a Change Triggers a New Conformity Assessment

A change to an already-deployed high-risk AI system constitutes a substantial modification under the EU AI Act if it meets both of the following conditions (**Article 3(23)**):
1. The change was not foreseen or planned in the initial conformity assessment carried out by the provider, and
2. As a result of the change, either:
 - The compliance of the AI system with the requirements set out in Chapter III, Section 2 (high-risk AI system requirements) is affected, or
 - The change results in a modification to the intended purpose for which the AI system was assessed.

If both conditions are met, the modification is substantial, and the system must undergo a new conformity assessment before it may be placed on the market or put into service again (**Article 43(4)**).

---

### Pre-Determined Changes: Exemption from Substantial Modification
The EU AI Act explicitly excludes pre-determined changes from the definition of substantial modification. If a change was foreseen and planned in the initial conformity assessment (e.g., documented in the technical documentation under **Annex IV**, point 1(h) as part of the […]), it does not trigger a new conformity assessment, even if it affects compliance or intended purpose (**Article 43(4)**, second subparagraph).

Key points on pre-determined changes:
- They must be documented in advance in the technical documentation (**Article 11(1)** and **Annex IV**).
- The provider must have assessed their impact on compliance and intended purpose during the initial conformity assessment.
- The system’s post-market monitoring plan (**Article 72**) should account for such changes to ensure ongoing compliance.

---

### Practical Implications
1. Substantial Modification → New Conformity Assessment Required
 - Example: A high-risk AI system for medical diagnosis (classified under **Article 6(1)** via **Annex I**, point 11 (MDR)) is modified to include a new diagnostic modality not covered in the original conformity assessment. This change affects compliance with Chapter III, Section 2 requirements (e.g., risk management under **Article 9** or data governance under **Article 10**) and is not pre-determined → substantial modification.
 - Legal effect: The provider must conduct a new conformity assessment (e.g., via **Annex VI** or **Annex VII**) before re-deploying the system.

2. Pre-Determined Change → No New Conformity Assessment
 - Example: A high-risk AI system for recruitment (classified under **Article 6(2)** via **Annex III**, point 4(a)) is updated to include a new filtering criterion, but this change was documented in the initial technical documentation and assessed for compliance. The change does not materially alter the intended purpose or compliance → not substantial.
 - Legal effect: The provider may implement the change without a new conformity assessment, provided the post-market monitoring system (**Article 72**) verifies ongoing compliance.

---

### Interpretive Guidance (Non-Binding)
The MDCG guidance (linked to **Article 43** and **Annex VI**/VII) clarifies that:
- For AI systems in the scope of the MDR/IVDR, the conformity assessment procedure is determined by the MDR/IVDR when the AI system is classified as high-risk under **Article 6(1)** (i.e., as a safety component or product under **Annex I**). In such cases, the MDR/IVDR conformity assessment prevails over the AI Act’s procedures.
- For high-risk AI systems classified under **Article 6(2)** (via **Annex III**), the AI Act’s conformity assessment procedures (**Annex VI** or **Annex VII**) apply unless the system is also covered by **Annex I** (e.g., medical devices), in which case the MDR/IVDR procedure takes precedence.

This guidance is non-binding but provides operational clarity for compliance.

---

### Key Takeaways
| Criterion | Substantial Modification | Pre-Determined Change |
|-----------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------|
| Foreseen in initial assessment? | No | Yes (documented in technical documentation) |
| Affects compliance? | Yes (with Chapter III, Section 2) | No (or impact already assessed) |
| Modifies intended purpose? | Yes | No (or change already accounted for) |
| Conformity assessment required? | Yes (new assessment under **Article 43(4)**) | No |
| Documentation requirement | Must update technical documentation (**Article 11**) | Must be pre-documented in technical documentation |

> ⚠ **ATTRIBUTION FLAG** — 1 of 1 quote(s) contain real regulatory text that does not belong to the provision they cite (or concatenate text from several provisions under one citation) and have been removed. Re-check which provision each obligation actually comes from.
> - “changes to the high-risk AI system and its performance”
